blocks.body: Types of interrogative sentences are different types of questions in English. Here you will learn how to form general questions (Yes/No Questions), special questions (Wh-Questions), alternative questions and questions, as well as questions to the subject.

• General questions - the answer is "yes" or "no": Do you like coffee?
  • An auxiliary verb is placed before the subject: Does he know? Can they swim?
  • With to be - simply invert: Is she ready? Are you happy?
  • Structure: Auxiliary + Subject + Main Verb + ...?
blocks.heading: General questions (Yes/No Questions)

blocks.body:
• Special questions — begin with question words: What, Where, When, Why, Who, How
  • Structure: Wh-word + Auxiliary + Subject + Main Verb + ...?
  • Examples: What do you want? Where are you going? When did it happen?
  • Questions to the subject - without an auxiliary verb: Who lives here? What happened?
blocks.heading: Special questions (Wh-Questions)

blocks.body:
• Alternative questions — a choice between options with "or": Do you prefer tea or coffee?
  • Structure as in general questions + or: Is it black or white?
  • Answer is one of the proposed options: Tea, please.
  • Can be more complicated: Would you like to go now or wait a bit?
blocks.heading: Alternative questions

blocks.body:
Topic Level Description
Yes/No Questions A1 General yes/no questions
Wh-Questions A1–A2 Special questions with what, where, when...
Subject Questions A2–B1 Questions to the subject (who, what)
Alternative Questions A2 Alternative questions with "or"
Question Tags B1 Chapter questions (isn't it?, don't you?)
Indirect Questions B1–B2 Indirect questions (Can you tell me...?)
blocks.heading: Topics in this section

blocks.body:
• Question Tags — a short question at the end of a sentence: You like tea, don't you?
  • Rule — affirmation + negative tag, or denial + positive tag.
  • Examples: She is happy, isn't she? They don't know, do they?
  • Usage — to confirm information or start a conversation.
blocks.heading: Question Tags

blocks.body:
🧠

Start with Yes/No questions - they are the easiest for beginners.

Learn question words (what, where, when, why, who, how) — they will help form special questions.

Remember word order: question word → auxiliary verb → subject → main verb.

For question tags use the opposite form: positive + negative or vice versa.
blocks.heading: Learning tips
